texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Time series clustering is essential in scientific applications, yet methods for functional time series, collections of infinite-dimensional curves treated as random elements in a Hilbert space, remain underdeveloped. This work presents clustering approaches for functional time series that combine the fuzzy C -medoids and fuzzy C -means procedures with a novel dissimilarity measure tailored for functional data. This dissimilarity is based on an extension of the quantile autocorrelation to the functional context. Our methods effectively groups time series with similar dependence structures, achieving high accuracy and computational efficiency in simulations. The practical utility of the approach is demonstrated through case studies on high-frequency financial stock data and multi-country age-specific mortality improvements.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Time series clustering partitions unlabeled time series into homogeneous groups based on a chosen similarity measure, aiming to reveal underlying structures without intensive model fitting. Its growing appeal stems from broad applications and challenges like selecting dissimilarity measures or handling dynamic shifts. For comprehensive reviews, see Aghabozorgi et al. (2015) and Maharaj et al. (2019).
texts.text: Time series clustering partitions unlabeled time series into homogeneous groups based on a chosen similarity measure, aiming to reveal underlying structures without intensive model fitting. Its growing appeal stems from broad applications and challenges like selecting dissimilarity measures or handling dynamic shifts. For comprehensive reviews, see Aghabozorgi et al. (2015) and Maharaj et al. (2019).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The choice of clustering technique depends on the time series characteristics and goals. Most methods focus on real-valued time series, often identifying distinct shapes using elastic dissimilarity measures like dynamic time warping (DTW) with K -means clustering (Ikotun et al. 2023). A key challenge is defining suitable prototypes, though Vera and Angulo (2023) shows that K -means can be applied without estimating them. Using DTW with K -means leads to generalized K -means, where standard properties may not hold (Vera and Mac´ ıas 2021, Vera and Angulo 2023). An alternative is K -medoids, which selects a cluster prototype (Aghabozorgi et al. 2015). Vera and Mac´ ıas (2021) used multidimensional scaling to apply standard K -means with any dissimilarity matrix. Recent work of Vera and Angulo (2023) and Vera and Rold´ an-Nofuentes (2024) has advanced robust DTW-based K -means methods for time series clustering.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Model-based clustering assumes predefined models for different groups (Maharaj 1999). Featurebased methods extract statistical features from each series and cluster them using a dissimilarity measure (L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2022, L´ opez-Oriona, D'Urso, Vilar and Lafuente-Rego 2022 a ). For multivariate data, dimensionality reduction, like PCA, is often applied before clustering (Li 2019).

Featurebased methods extract statistical features from each series and cluster them using a dissimilarity measure (L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2022, L´ opez-Oriona, D'Urso, Vilar and Lafuente-Rego 2022 a ). For multivariate data, dimensionality reduction, like PCA, is often applied before clustering (Li 2019).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clustering methods yield either hard (crisp) or soft (fuzzy) outcomes. Hard clustering assigns each series to one group, creating disjoint clusters, while soft clustering allows partial membership across multiple groups, offering more flexibility and richer insights (Miyamoto et al. 2008). Some time series switch between clusters over time (D'Urso and Maharaj 2009), which fuzzy clustering effectively captures. It also offers computational efficiency (McBratney and Moore 1985), works with distribution-free methods, and identifies a 'second-best' cluster (Everitt et al. 2011).
texts.text: Clustering methods yield either hard (crisp) or soft (fuzzy) outcomes. Hard clustering assigns each series to one group, creating disjoint clusters, while soft clustering allows partial membership across multiple groups, offering more flexibility and richer insights (Miyamoto et al. 2008). Some time series switch between clusters over time (D'Urso and Maharaj 2009), which fuzzy clustering effectively captures. It also offers computational efficiency (McBratney and Moore 1985), works with distribution-free methods, and identifies a 'second-best' cluster (Everitt et al. 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) functional data analysis, clustering is often combined with dimension reduction to address the curse of dimensionality. Tarpey and Kinateder (2003) and Garcia-Escudero and Gordaliza (2005) approximated data using fewer basis functions before clustering the coefficients, while Bouveyron et al. (2015) used a functional mixture model for bike-sharing data. Tzeng et al. (2018) introduced a dissimilarity measure based on smoothing splines. Further work includes Chamroukhi et al. (2024) on mixtures-of-experts for functional data and Bouveyron and Jacques (2011), later expanded by Jacques and Preda (2014), on high-dimensional clustering assuming Gaussian principal component scores. For a review, see Chamroukhi and Nguyen (2019) and Zhang and Parnell (2023).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clustering functional time series has been studied only by van Delft and Dette (2021) and Tang et al. (2022) (the former in a nonstationary context), highlighting the need for specialized algorithms. Challenges include: (i) classical methods do not handle functional data (Ramsay and Silverman 2005), (ii) difficulties defining serial dependence (Valencia et al. 2019), and (iii) high-dimensionality (Kim, Kokoszka and Rice 2024), which may explain the limited research.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The high dimensionality and complexity of temporal data make the fuzzy approach effective for clustering functional time series. Switching behavior, common in real-valued time series (D'Urso and Maharaj 2009), also occurs in functional time series but is more complex due to intricate serial dependence structures in high-dimensional curves. This paper introduces soft clustering procedures for functional time series to: (i) group series with similar underlying processes, (ii) achieve high clustering accuracy across diverse datasets, and (iii) efficiently compute the clustering partition. We combine traditional fuzzy clustering with a novel dissimilarity measure tailored for functional data.
texts.text: The high dimensionality and complexity of temporal data make the fuzzy approach effective for clustering functional time series. Switching behavior, common in real-valued time series (D'Urso and Maharaj 2009), also occurs in functional time series but is more complex due to intricate serial dependence structures in high-dimensional curves. This paper introduces soft clustering procedures for functional time series to: (i) group series with similar underlying processes, (ii) achieve high clustering accuracy across diverse datasets, and (iii) efficiently compute the clustering partition. We combine traditional fuzzy clustering with a novel dissimilarity measure tailored for functional data.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We define a dissimilarity measure for functional time series. Since our objective is to cluster the series according to their generating processes, the metric is based on certain quantities, giving information about the serial dependence structures. The extracted features rely on a measure of serial dependence, which can be considered as a functional extension of the standard quantile autocorrelation. Thus, the considered measure inherits the nice properties of the latter quantity in a clustering context, as the ability to identify complex dependence patterns that standard correlationbased methods are unable to detect or the low computational complexity (Lafuente-Rego and Vilar 2016).

Since our objective is to cluster the series according to their generating processes, the metric is based on certain quantities, giving information about the serial dependence structures. The extracted features rely on a measure of serial dependence, which can be considered as a functional extension of the standard quantile autocorrelation. Thus, the considered measure inherits the nice properties of the latter quantity in a clustering context, as the ability to identify complex dependence patterns that standard correlationbased methods are unable to detect or the low computational complexity (Lafuente-Rego and Vilar 2016).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proposed distance is used with fuzzy C -medoids (Krishnapuram et al. 1999, L´ opez-Oriona, D'Urso, Vilar and Lafuente-Rego 2022 b ) and fuzzy C -means (D'Urso and Maharaj 2009) algorithms to assign gradual memberships to clusters. The main difference is that C -medoids uses time series as prototypes (medoids), while C -means uses virtual prototypes (centroids). We evaluate the clustering techniques through simulation experiments with various stochastic processes and compare them to other methods. The techniques are applied to intraday stock returns and age-specific mortality improvement rates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 introduces a serial dependence measure for functional processes, used to construct a dissimilarity for functional time series. Section 3 designs clustering algorithms combining the proposed dissimilarity with existing methods. Section 4 evaluates the performance through numerical experiments, comparing competing procedures. Section 5 applies the methods to intraday stock returns and age-specific mortality improvement rates. Finally, Section 6 presents conclusions and potential extensions.

texts.orig: Remark 1 . On the importance of thresholds and their choice . The thresholds β and β ′ in γ ( τ , τ ′ , l , β , β ′ ) evaluate serial dependence by considering the 'proportion of time' functions X t and X t + l are above or below quantile curves. This is natural in the functional setting, as serial dependence can vary across different regions of the function's domain. This extends the classical quantile autocovariance function (LafuenteRego and Vilar 2016) to the functional setting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 2 . Feature-based dissimilarities for functional time series . The metric ̂ d FQA is a feature-based dissimilarity (Fulcher 2018), where identifying latent clustering depends on selecting appropriate features. Once suitable features are chosen, these metrics can compare series of unequal lengths. Calculating features that capture serial dependence in functional time series can be computationally demanding, especially when using measures like autocorrelation (Kokoszka et al. 2017). In contrast, FQA-derived features are more efficient, relying on simple estimations and pairwise comparisons.

(2022), which relies on a multilevel functional data model applied to functional panel data. Although this technique cannot be directly associated with a distance measure, we will denote it as ̂ d TSY, which constitutes a hard clustering approach that automatically determines the number of clusters using a specific measure of within-cluster dispersion. In any case, ̂ d TSY can still be compared with the proposed approach in a reasonable manner in some of the simulation experiments (refer to Section 4.2.1). texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dissimilarities ̂ d FACF and ̂ d FSACF are very natural competitors for the distance ̂ d FQA, as they are both based on well-established autocorrelation measures for functional time series. The metrics ̂ d Km and ̂ d Ki rely on a direct extension to the temporal setting of a Kendall correlation coefficient between functional data. This dependence measure employs functional preorders to quantify the association between two sets of curves in a robust manner, and its effectiveness has been demonstrated in several real applications (Valencia et al. 2019). Thus, ̂ d Km and ̂ d Ki are robust dissimilarities that can detect differences between functional time series in terms of dependence structures, as ̂ d FQA. Therefore, it is also reasonable to compare these dissimilarities with the proposed distance. Comparisons between the clustering procedures based on the five metrics mentioned above provide insight into the ability of the corresponding dependence measures to discriminate between underlying dynamic patterns. In contrast, the model-based procedure ̂ d TSY follows a completely different approach (e.g., it is not based on fuzzy C -medoids or fuzzy C -means), but its inclusion as a benchmark method in the comparisons is justified by the fact that ̂ d TSY is, to the best of our knowledge, the only method available in the literature for clustering multiple functional time series.

texts.orig: 4.2 Simulation settings and results
texts.text: 4.2 Simulation settings and results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of the clustering approaches based on ̂ d FQA was evaluated through an extensive simulation study with different functional processes. Two evaluation systems were used: the first assesses the methods' ability to detect well-separated groups in four-cluster scenarios, while the second evaluates their ability to identify two clusters and detect the fuzzy behavior of an ambiguous time series. In particular, a specific threshold must be fixed to determine when a given time series is assumed to belong to a given cluster.
texts.text: The performance of the clustering approaches based on ̂ d FQA was evaluated through an extensive simulation study with different functional processes. Two evaluation systems were used: the first assesses the methods' ability to detect well-separated groups in four-cluster scenarios, while the second evaluates their ability to identify two clusters and detect the fuzzy behavior of an ambiguous time series. In particular, a specific threshold must be fixed to determine when a given time series is assumed to belong to a given cluster.

texts.orig: The numerical experiments were performed in the following way. Given a scenario, five functional time series were simulated from each generating process, thus giving rise to a dataset of 20 series to be subject to the clustering techniques. Two values of the series length were used, namely T ∈ { 200, 600 } . Anumber of p = 100 discretization points evenly spaced on [ 0, 1 ] was employed in all cases. Five different values were considered for the fuzziness parameter, namely m ∈ { 1.2, 1.4, 1.6, 1.8, 2 } . Similar grids have been employed in related works (D'Urso and Maharaj 2009, L´ opez-Oriona and Vilar 2021, L´ opez-Oriona, Weiß and Vilar 2023). Note that, when m = 1, we obtain the crisp version of the corresponding clustering models, while very large values of m result in a substantial degree of overlap between groups.

texts.orig: Two hundred simulations were performed for a given scenario and specific values for m and T . In each trial, the fuzzy C -medoids and fuzzy C -means methods based on ̂ d FQA, ̂ d FACF, ̂ d FSACF, ̂ d Km , and ̂ d Ki were executed using the specific value of m as input. The parameter C was set as C = 4 (the number of different generating processes). The computation of the three metrics was carried out by using the collections of lags L = { 1, 2 } and L = { 1 } in Scenarios 1 and 2, respectively, hence considering the maximum number of defining lags in each case. In addition, for the computation of ̂ d FQA, the collection T was set as { 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 } (see Section 3.2). Moreover, we also performed the analyses described in this section by considering different choices for the sets mentioned above, but no significant improvements were found.

texts.orig: The method ̂ d TSY cannot be directly compared with those remaining. On the one hand, since ̂ d TSY constitutes a hard clustering approach and the current scenarios consider underlying hard partitions, this method is expected to naturally lead to higher values of ARIF and JIF than the alternative techniques. On the other hand, ̂ d TSY automatically determines the number of clusters in its first stage, which is a disadvantage with respect to the approaches based on ̂ d FQA, ̂ d FACF, ̂ d FSACF, ̂ d Km , and ̂ d Ki , since the latter methods use the true value C = 4 as input. To carry out fair comparisons, we considered only the trials in which ̂ d TSY selected the true number of clusters ( C = 4).

texts.orig: The mean values of the clustering quality indexes for the fuzzy C -medoids procedures based on the five dissimilarity measures are presented in the upper and lower parts of Table 1 for Scenarios 1 and 2, respectively. Note that for larger values of m , the methods exhibit lower clustering accuracy. This is expected, as increasing the fuzziness parameter spreads the membership degrees more evenly across groups, leading to a fuzzier partition and worse performance measures. To rigorously compare the five distances, pairwise paired t -tests were conducted using the 200 simulation trials.

texts.orig: In each setting, the alternative hypotheses stated that the mean ARIF (JIF) value of a given method is greater than the mean ARIF (JIF) value of its counterpart. Bonferroni corrections were applied to the set of p -values associated with each scenario, clustering quality index, and value of T . An asterisk was incorporated in Table 1 if the corresponding method was: (i) no significantly worse than any other method for a significance level of 0.01, and (ii) significantly more effective than those without an asterisk for a significance level of 0.01. The method ̂ d TSY achieves substantially lower scores than the remaining approaches in most cases. In Scenario 1, ̂ d TSY reaches an ARIF (JIF) of 0.23 (0.30) and 0.27 (0.35) for T = 200 and T = 600, respectively. In Scenario 2, it achieves an ARIF (JIF) of 0.36 (0.35) and 0.41 (0.44) for T = 200 and T = 600, respectively. These scores suggest that this technique is not suitable for clustering when the differences are driven by the dependence structures of the functional time series. For this reason, the results associated with ̂ d TSY are omitted in subsequent analyses.

texts.orig: The performance measure previously described requires the selection of a fixed threshold to determine when a time series must be placed in a given group. In this regard, we used a threshold of 0.7, that is, a given functional time series was assigned to a specific cluster if its membership degree in that cluster was greater than 0.7. Note that similar rules based on thresholds have already been employed for the evaluation of time series clustering methods (L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2022).

texts.orig: The curves in Figure 1 corroborate that the parameter m substantially influences clustering accuracy. The performance for all methods is bad when very small and large values of this parameter are considered, which was expected since fuzzy solutions with all membership degrees close to 1 or 0.5 are produced in such cases. On the other hand, when m is in the middle of the range, better clustering accuracy is achieved by the procedures. In Scenario 3, the proposed metric attains similar overall results compared to the dissimilarities based on Kendall autocorrelations, even though the models in this scenario are characterized by linear dependence structures. Generally, better success rates are obtained for these three distances when increasing the value of T . On the other hand, the metrics ̂ d FACF and ̂ d FSACF achieve success rates of 0 for both values of the series length (the red color can not be seen in the corresponding graphs because the brown and red lines are completely overlapping). In Scenario 4, the dissimilarity ̂ d FQA gets substantially better results than all the competing metrics. In particular, ̂ d FACF, ̂ d FSACF, ̂ d Km , and ̂ d Ki attain very poor rates of correct classification even when long time series ( T = 600) are considered. The above analyses confirm that a proper choice of the fuzziness parameter is essential. However, this topic is not addressed in this

texts.orig: In summary, the experiments in Section 4.2 show that clustering approaches based on ̂ d FQA: (i) perform competitively with linear time series, even outperforming Kendall autocorrelation-based methods in the presence of ambiguous series, and (ii) achieve significantly higher accuracy when the underlying clusters exhibit complex forms of functional dependence. An analysis of the efficiency of the clustering approaches examined throughout Section 4 was also performed. The results are shown in Section 8 of the Supplement.

The procedures based on the proposed metric achieve acceptable values for the fuzzy clustering indexes (ARIF and JIF), which was expected given the fuzzy partition in Table 3. The metrics ̂ d FACF, ̂ d FSACF, and ̂ d Ki attain better scores than ̂ d FQA for both indexes when the fuzzy C -medoids models are considered. However, for both clustering approaches, ̂ d FQA reaches substantially higher values than all the remaining dissimilarities in terms of the crisp clustering indexes (ARI and JI). These results corroborate that ̂ d FQA is capable of detecting some structure in the functional time series dataset having an actual meaning in terms of industrial sectors.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Conclusions
texts.text: 6 Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We introduced a distance measure for functional time series based on functional quantile autocorrelation, an extension of the quantile autocorrelation for real-valued time series. This dissimilarity was used with fuzzy C -medoids and fuzzy C -means algorithms to design clustering techniques for functional time series. These methods create a soft partition, allowing time series to have varying membership degrees in clusters, handling uncertainty in temporal datasets. The techniques involve hyperparameters, which can be selected using reasonable heuristic rules.
texts.text: We introduced a distance measure for functional time series based on functional quantile autocorrelation, an extension of the quantile autocorrelation for real-valued time series. This dissimilarity was used with fuzzy C -medoids and fuzzy C -means algorithms to design clustering techniques for functional time series. These methods create a soft partition, allowing time series to have varying membership degrees in clusters, handling uncertainty in temporal datasets. The techniques involve hyperparameters, which can be selected using reasonable heuristic rules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proposed clustering procedures were evaluated through simulations with well-defined and fuzzy group settings, using various functional processes. We compared them with four clustering algorithms based on functional autocorrelations and one using functional panel data modeling. The proposed techniques: (i) yield competitive results on linear time series, though they are generally outperformed by functional Kendall autocorrelation-based methods in this context, and (ii) significantly outperform alternative approaches when the clusters exhibit complex functional dependence, while also being computationally efficient. We recommend the proposed methods for predominantly nonlinear time series and functional Kendall autocorrelation-based methods for mostly linear series.
texts.text: The proposed clustering procedures were evaluated through simulations with well-defined and fuzzy group settings, using various functional processes. We compared them with four clustering algorithms based on functional autocorrelations and one using functional panel data modeling. The proposed techniques: (i) yield competitive results on linear time series, though they are generally outperformed by functional Kendall autocorrelation-based methods in this context, and (ii) significantly outperform alternative approaches when the clusters exhibit complex functional dependence, while also being computationally efficient. We recommend the proposed methods for predominantly nonlinear time series and functional Kendall autocorrelation-based methods for mostly linear series.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two applications demonstrated the proposed methods' effectiveness, showing that: (i) the proposed measure of serial dependence reveals key insights into the nature of the analyzed time series, and (ii) examining the membership degrees in the clustering solutions offers practitioners a deeper understanding of the underlying structure of the time series.
texts.text: Two applications demonstrated the proposed methods' effectiveness, showing that: (i) the proposed measure of serial dependence reveals key insights into the nature of the analyzed time series, and (ii) examining the membership degrees in the clustering solutions offers practitioners a deeper understanding of the underlying structure of the time series.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work can be extended in several ways: 1) Improving hyperparameter selection for greater accuracy and efficiency. 2) Developing robust extensions using metric, noise, and trimmed approaches (L´ opez-Oriona, D'Urso, Vilar and Lafuente-Rego 2022 a ). 3) Incorporating spatial components to handle spatially structured functional time series (L´ opez-Oriona, D'Urso, Vilar and Lafuente-Rego 2022 b ). 4) Defining a spectral counterpart of functional quantile autocorrelation for frequency analysis. 5) Studying its asymptotic behavior to construct statistical tests.

texts.text: S3.1 Selection of the set L

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The set of lags ( L ) can be chosen by employing the selection procedure proposed by L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso (2023) (see Section 3.4), adapted to the functional setting. This mechanism uses a criterion that consists of determining the significant lags for each functional time series in the collection and then fixing a maximum lag for all of them. In particular, given the set S = { X ( 1 ) , X ( 2 ) , . . . , X ( n ) } , we suggest to select the collection L through the following steps:
texts.text: The set of lags ( L ) can be chosen by employing the selection procedure proposed by L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso (2023) (see Section 3.4), adapted to the functional setting. This mechanism uses a criterion that consists of determining the significant lags for each functional time series in the collection and then fixing a maximum lag for all of them. In particular, given the set S = { X ( 1 ) , X ( 2 ) , . . . , X ( n ) } , we suggest to select the collection L through the following steps:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Fix α > 0 and a maximum lag L Max ∈ N . Using the Bonferroni's adjustment for multiple comparisons, compute the corrected significance level α ′ = α / ( nL Max ) .
texts.text: Fix α > 0 and a maximum lag L Max ∈ N . Using the Bonferroni's adjustment for multiple comparisons, compute the corrected significance level α ′ = α / ( nL Max ) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. For each series X ( i ) ∈ S :
texts.text: For each series X ( i ) ∈ S :

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.1. Determine the set L i formed for all the lags l i ∈ { 1, 2, . . . , L Max } for which the null hypothesis of functional serial independence is rejected according to a t -test based on the so-called distance correlation (Sz´ ekely and Rizzo 2013).
texts.text: 2.1. Determine the set L i formed for all the lags l i ∈ { 1, 2, . . . , L Max } for which the null hypothesis of functional serial independence is rejected according to a t -test based on the so-called distance correlation (Sz´ ekely and Rizzo 2013).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.2. Select the lag Li ∈ L i associated with the lowest p -value.
texts.text: 2.2. Select the lag Li ∈ L i associated with the lowest p -value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the above process, the Bonferroni correction is used to address the problem of multiple comparisons, since nL Max tests are simultaneously carried out. In Step 2.2, the most significant lag is selected to avoid including redundant features, since dependence at lower lags often leads to (less strong) dependence at higher lags. In Step 3, L is constructed considering all lags between 1 and L ∗ . Note that L ∗ is necessarily a significant lag for one or several functional time series, although indeed, some series may not display significant serial dependence at L ∗ or lower lags. However, this is not an issue because the corresponding estimated features are expected to be close to zero for these series. As an example, according to the selection mechanism described above, if some of the functional time series in the collection exhibit primarily weekly dependence, then Li = 7 for some i , and the set L will necessarily include lag 7. The t -test based on the distance correlation is implemented via the function dcor.xy() of the package fda.usc (Febrero-Bande and Oviedo de la Fuente 2012).

texts.text: S3.2 Selection of the sets T and B

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the collection of quantile levels ( T ), we suggest using three quantiles of levels 0.1, 0.5, and 0.9, since several works on quantile-based time series clustering (Lafuente-Rego and Vilar 2016, Vilar et al. 2018, L´ opez-Oriona and Vilar 2021, L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2022) have shown that: 1. (i) such choice often leads to a high clustering effectiveness, and (ii) considering more quantile levels usually results in nonsignificant improvements. In fact, the numerical experiments performed through Section 4 in the main text (in which different choices for T are considered) corroborate that T = { 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 } is also a reasonable choice in the functional setting. With respect to the set of thresholds ( B ), we suggest to consider τ i 1 = β j 1 and τ i 2 = β j 2 (see Remark 1 in the main text) in the framework of Section 2.2 in the main text. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Minimizing the numerator in the previous expression, which measures the compactness of the fuzzy partition, is the main goal of the fuzzy C -means model based on ̂ d FQA when m = 2. The denominator measures the degree of separation between groups. Specifically, the smaller the numerator, the more compact the clustering partition, whereas the greater the denominator, the larger the degree of separation between groups. Thus, the Xie-Beni index combines the concepts of compactness and separation so that a lower index value indicates a better clustering solution.

texts.orig: Note that the definition of the Xie-Beni index is independent of the ̂ d FQA-based clustering algorithm used to obtain U . If the fuzzy C -means procedure is employed, the resulting centroids ( ̂ ρ ( c ) , c = 1, . . . , C ) can be obtained directly from the output of the algorithm. On the contrary, if the fuzzy C -medoids method is used, the centroids can still be easily calculated using the corresponding fuzzy solution as input to (6), or an alternative definition of the Xie-Beni index using the medoids as prototypes could be considered. Some works on clustering of time series have employed similar criteria to select the parameters C and m (L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2022, L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2023).

texts.text: S4 Two-dimensional scaling representations for the generating processes in Scenarios 1 and 2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As an exploratory step, we carried out a metric two-dimensional scaling (2DS) based on the distance ̂ d FQA in the context of Section 4.2.1 in the main text. This tool is often used to obtain an idea of the capability of a given metric to discriminate between specific underlying groups. Given a pairwise dissimilarity matrix, D = ( Dij ) 1 ≤ i , j ≤ n , a 2DS looks for the set of points { ( ai , bi ) , i = 1, . . . , n } minimizing the following expression (called stress):
texts.text: As an exploratory step, we carried out a metric two-dimensional scaling (2DS) based on the distance ̂ d FQA in the context of Section 4.2.1 in the main text. This tool is often used to obtain an idea of the capability of a given metric to discriminate between specific underlying groups. Given a pairwise dissimilarity matrix, D = ( Dij ) 1 ≤ i , j ≤ n , a 2DS looks for the set of points { ( ai , bi ) , i = 1, . . . , n } minimizing the following expression (called stress):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: √ √ √ √ ∑ n i = j = 1 ( ∥ ∥ ( ai , bi ) -( aj , bj ) ∥ ∥ -Dij ) 2 ∑ n i = j = 1 D 2 ij .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The goal is to represent the distances Dij in terms of Euclidean distances into a two-dimensional space so that the original distances are preserved as well as possible. The lower the value of the stress function, the more reliable the 2DS configuration. This way, a 2DS plot provides a valuable visual representation of how the elements are located with respect to each other according to the original distance.
texts.text: The goal is to represent the distances Dij in terms of Euclidean distances into a two-dimensional space so that the original distances are preserved as well as possible. The lower the value of the stress function, the more reliable the 2DS configuration. texts.label: text
texts.orig: To obtain informative plots, 50 functional time series of length T = 1000 were simulated from each generating model. The 2DS was performed for each set of 200 functional time series by calculating the pairwise distance matrix based on ̂ d FQA. We considered the set of lags L = { 1, 2 } in Scenario 1 and L = { 1 } in Scenario 2. Figure 3 contains the corresponding plots, in which the different colors correspond to different generating processes. To assess the quality of the embeddings, we computed the corresponding stress measures and the p -values associated with a permutation test applied to the corresponding dissimilarity matrices (Mair et al. 2016). The null hypothesis of this test is that the configuration is obtained from a random permutation of the dissimilarities. We obtained stress measures of 1.26% and 1.55% for the 2DS associated with Scenarios 1 and 2, respectively, and p -values below 0.01 in both cases. Note that both stress measures are below 5%, thus indicating an excellent goodness-of-fit (Dugard et al. 2010), and both p -values are clearly significant. Therefore, the previous results indicate that the graphs in Figure 3 provide a reliable summary of the latent representations according to the distance ̂ d FQA.

texts.orig: The results regarding the fuzzy C -means approaches in Scenarios 1 and 2 are provided in Table 6 using a format analogous to one employed in Table 1 in the main text. For the dissimilarity ̂ d FQA, the fuzzy C -means model results in a slightly lower clustering accuracy than the fuzzy C -medoids model for low values of m . On the contrary, when m ∈ { 1.6, 1.8, 2 } , the former model generally leads to higher scores. For the remaining metrics, fuzzy C -means results in a worse clustering effectiveness in almost all cases. The only exception involves the metric ̂ d FACF in Scenario 2 with T = 200. Note that, in this scenario, the fuzzy C -means model based on ̂ d FQA achieves a significantly higher clustering accuracy than the remaining fuzzy C -means approaches in all cases.

texts.orig: Note that the practical implementation of ̂ d FQA requires fixing the collection of quantile levels T (that also determines the collection of thresholds according to Section 3.2 in the main text), which was set to T = { 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 } in the original experiments. In this regard, in order to analyze the influence of T in the performance of the ̂ d FQA-based clustering algorithms, we decided to replicate the original simulations considering T = { 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9 } for the computation of the proposed metric. The average values of the clustering indexes for this extended set of quantile levels are given in Table 7 for the fuzzy C -medoids model, and in Table 8 for the fuzzy C -means model. The results indicate that, in most cases, including more quantile levels does not necessarily lead to better clustering accuracy. This is consistent with several works on quantile-based time series clustering (Lafuente-Rego and Vilar 2016, Vilar et al. 2018, L´ opez-Oriona and Vilar 2021, L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2022), where it is shown that considering the set T = { 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 } is usually enough to reach an optimal clustering accuracy.
texts.text: Note that the practical implementation of ̂ d FQA requires fixing the collection of quantile levels T (that also determines the collection of thresholds according to Section 3.2 in the main text), which was set to T = { 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 } in the original experiments. In this regard, in order to analyze the influence of T in the performance of the ̂ d FQA-based clustering algorithms, we decided to replicate the original simulations considering T = { 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9 } for the computation of the proposed metric. The average values of the clustering indexes for this extended set of quantile levels are given in Table 7 for the fuzzy C -medoids model, and in Table 8 for the fuzzy C -means model. The results indicate that, in most cases, including more quantile levels does not necessarily lead to better clustering accuracy. This is consistent with several works on quantile-based time series clustering (Lafuente-Rego and Vilar 2016, Vilar et al. 2018, L´ opez-Oriona and Vilar 2021, L´ opez-Oriona, Vilar and D'Urso 2022), where it is shown that considering the set T = { 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 } is usually enough to reach an optimal clustering accuracy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to analyze the efficiency of the clustering approaches examined throughout Section 4 in the main text, we present the runtime of the corresponding programs used for the experiments in Scenario 1. For all the methods except for ̂ d TSY, we consider the runtime associated with the fuzzy C -medoids approach. Given a method and a value for T , we report the CPU runtime required to complete the clustering task (for the five values of m ) for the 200 simulation trials. The computer used to run the programs was a MacBook Pro with an Apple M2 Pro chip and 16 GB of RAM. The programs were coded and executed in the software (R Core Team 2024) (version 4.3.1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The runtime (minutes) associated with each method is provided in Table 12. The fuzzy C -medoids method based on ̂ d FQA is associated with the lowest runtime for both values of T . In contrast, ̂ d TSY is by far associated with the highest runtime. Additionally, the computation time of the proposed approach barely increases with T . On the contrary, the runtime associated with distances ̂ d Km and ̂ d Ki shows a substantial increase with T , which is expected, since the feature extraction mechanism of these metrics entails a number of ∑ L k = 1 ( T -l k 2 ) comparisons. Among these two dissimilarities, ̂ d Ki is computationally heavier due to the integration involved in the definition of ≺ i . It may not be feasible to run the method based on ̂ d Ki in databases containing many time series of moderate to large lengths. The runtime associated with the dissimilarities ̂ d FACF and ̂ d FSACF increases linearly with T , but the former metric implies considerably heavier computations than the latter.

texts.text: S9 Two-dimensional scaling representation for the financial application

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the context of Section 5.1 in the main text, as an exploratory exercise, we constructed a 2DS plot based on ̂ d FQA, which was computed considering the chosen values for T and L . This allowed us to obtain a ̂ d FQA-based representation of the 40 financial series in a two-dimensional plane. The corresponding plot is shown in Figure 5, where the points are colored according to the sector associated with each company. The goodness-of-fit of the 2DS was evaluated through the stress measure and the p -value for the permutation test applied to the corresponding dissimilarity matrix (as in Section S4), which resulted 5.89% and less than 0.01, respectively. Note that the stress measure is very close to 5%, the threshold below which the 2DS representation is considered satisfactory (Dugard et al. 2010), and the p -value is clearly significant. Therefore, the plot in Figure 5 provides a reliable representation of the underlying structure according to the distance ̂ d FQA.

texts.orig: S10 Clustering age-specific mortality improvement rates
texts.text: S10 Clustering age-specific mortality improvement rates

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider age-specific mortality rates in 41 countries over several years. The mortality rates are the ratios of death counts to population exposure in the relevant year for a given age interval. We examine age groupings ranging from 0 to 110 in single years of age, with the last age group including all ages of 110 and above. The data were sourced from the Human Mortality Database (2024). As the available sample period differs among the 41 countries, and there are some countries with only a few years of available data, we decided to consider only the countries for which we have mortality data since at least 1960. For such countries, our sample period is from 1960 till the last year of available data, which varies among countries. Among the original countries, 33 were selected, namely Australia (AUS), Austria (AUT), Belarus (BLR), Belgium (BEL), Bulgaria (BGR), Canada (CAN), Czech Republic (CZE), Denmark (DNK), Estonia (EST), Finland (FIN), France (FRA), Hungary (HUN), Iceland (ISL), Ireland (IRL), Italy (ITA), Japan (JPN), Latvia (LVA), Lithuania (LTU), Luxembourg (LUX), the Netherlands (NLD), New Zealand (NZL), Norway (NOR), Poland (POL), Portugal (PRT), Russia (RUS), Slovakia (SVK), Spain (ESP), Sweden (SWE), Switzerland (CHE), Taiwan (TWN), the United Kingdom (GBR), Ukraine (UKR), and the United States (USA).

texts.orig: The clustering partition produced by the fuzzy C -medoids algorithm is given in the left part of Table 13, where the bold font has the same meaning as in the above application. As expected from the selected value for m , the highest membership degree is close to one for almost all countries, thus suggesting an underlying clustering configuration with a low degree of fuzziness. Cluster C 1 contains many European countries along with AUS, NZL, and TWN. Cluster C 2 includes BLR, RUS, and UKR, along with the North American countries CAN and USA, the Baltic states EST, LVA, and LTU, and SVK. It is worth highlighting that this second group makes sense from a geographical and political point of view. For instance, until 1991, the six neighboring countries BLR, RUS, UKR, EST, LVA, and LTU belonged to the same state, the Soviet Union. Therefore, it is not surprising that the mortality improvement rates in these countries show similar behavior over the last 60 years. The only country with membership degrees substantially spread out is JPN, the mortality improvement rates of which share some patterns with the series in both groups. The clustering partition associated with the fuzzy C -means procedure is provided in the left part of Table 14.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The clustering partitions associated with the male and female populations for the fuzzy C -medoids model are given in the middle and right parts of Table 13, respectively. Note that even though the clustering solution for the male population involves the same number of groups as that for the total population, both partitions are substantially different. In fact, the solution for the male population includes a cluster C 1 containing all the countries except for ISL, which is the medoid of cluster C 2, thus indicating that the male mortality data of this country are dramatically different from the rest in terms of serial dependence patterns. On the other hand, the solution for the female population is quite difficult to interpret since it involves six groups. The fuzzy solutions for the male and female populations regarding the fuzzy C -means model are given in the middle and right parts of Table 14, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The weighted averages of the corresponding FQA-based features for the male and female data associated with the fuzzy C -medoids model are provided in the bottom part of Table 15 and Table 16, respectively. Note that, for the male population, some quantities associated with cluster C 2 are quite large in absolute value, implying that the male mortality data of ISL exhibit a substantially stronger degree of serial dependence than the ones displayed by its counterparts. On the other hand, for the female population, the six clusters are associated with different dependence patterns, and, in all cases, the groups display a stronger degree of dependence at the first lag ( l = 1) than at the second lag ( l = 2).
